result:
:::writing
At the edge of a small, forgettable town, there was a vending machine that never ran out. It stood alone beside a flickering streetlamp, humming softly like it knew something no one else did. People didn’t talk about it much, but they used it—late at night, when curiosity feels braver than reason.

The strange thing wasn’t that it always worked. It was that it gave you exactly what you didn’t know you needed.

A tired nurse once pressed a button for coffee and received a folded note that simply said, “Call him.” She did. It changed everything. A teenager fed in coins for soda and got a bus ticket instead—one way, leaving town. He stared at it for a long time before pocketing it, heart racing with something unfamiliar: possibility.

No labels matched the items. Buttons shifted slightly between visits. Some swore the machine watched them, waiting, deciding.

An old man came every Thursday. He never hesitated. One coin, one press. Sometimes he got ordinary things—a candy bar, a newspaper. Sometimes he got nothing at all, just the quiet whir of the machine thinking. He always nodded, as if even nothing was an answer.

One night, the streetlamp went dark. The machine stopped humming.

By morning, it was gone.

In its place stood nothing but a patch of clean pavement and, oddly, a single coin left behind. No one claimed it. No one spent it.

But every now and then, someone passing by swears they hear a faint hum, like a question waiting to be asked.
:::

:::writing
At the edge of a small, forgettable town, there was a vending machine that never ran out. It stood alone beside a flickering streetlamp, humming softly like it knew something no one else did. People didn’t talk about it much, but they used it—late at night, when curiosity feels braver than reason.

The strange thing wasn’t that it always worked. It was that it gave you exactly what you didn’t know you needed.

A tired nurse once pressed a button for coffee and received a folded note that simply said, “Call him.” She did. It changed everything. A teenager fed in coins for soda and got a bus ticket instead—one way, leaving town. He stared at it for a long time before pocketing it, heart racing with something unfamiliar: possibility.

No labels matched the items. Buttons shifted slightly between visits. Some swore the machine watched them, waiting, deciding.

An old man came every Thursday. He never hesitated. One coin, one press. Sometimes he got ordinary things—a candy bar, a newspaper. Sometimes he got nothing at all, just the quiet whir of the machine thinking. He always nodded, as if even nothing was an answer.

One night, the streetlamp went dark. The machine stopped humming.

By morning, it was gone.

In its place stood nothing but a patch of clean pavement and, oddly, a single coin left behind. No one claimed it. No one spent it.

But every now and then, someone passing by swears they hear a faint hum, like a question waiting to be asked.
:::

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
